title: SDNY Judge Castel Orders ICE to Halt Courthouse Arrests; Agency Had 'No Justification'
excerpt: After ICE attorneys admitted they had no legal justification for courthouse arrests at New York immigration courts, U.S. District Judge P. Kevin Castel issued a stay prohibiting ICE from conducting civil immigration arrests at immigration courthouses in New York City.
content:
On May 18, 2026, U.S. District Judge P. Kevin Castel of the Southern District of New York granted a stay prohibiting ICE from conducting civil immigration arrests at immigration courts in New York City — after ICE attorneys conceded they had no legal justification for the ongoing arrests.

  • The ruling followed the March DOJ admission that the 2025 ICE memo used to authorize courthouse arrests did not apply to immigration courts.
  • The ACLU noted that ICE officers had been arresting immigrants during mandatory court hearings despite having acknowledged — in open court — the absence of any lawful basis for doing so.
  • The order required ICE to revert to a Biden-era policy allowing courthouse arrests only in narrow circumstances: national security threats, direct pursuit of a subject, or situations where arrest elsewhere was impossible.
  • The ruling was part of a broader wave of federal litigation challenging ICE's courthouse enforcement practices across multiple jurisdictions in 2026.

The admission that ICE had been conducting mass arrests at courthouses without legal justification represented a significant documented failure of both policy guidance and officer training on lawful arrest authority.
mediaPlaceholder: Court order and ACLU press release documenting ICE's admission of no legal justification
imageAlt: SDNY Judge Castel Orders ICE to Halt Courthouse Arrests; Agency Had 'No Justification'
